title: json grep: find paths whose key or value matches a regex
description: Add `sak json grep <pattern> [files...]` that searches a JSON document for paths whose key or value matches a regex. Options: --key (match keys only), --value (match values only), --type <t> (filter by JSON type). Output: 'path<TAB>value' lines. Useful for finding all api_* keys, all string values containing an email, etc.

title: Add config grep command (structural search)
description:
Add `sak config grep <pattern> [files...]` — search a config document for keys or values matching a regex, returning the matching paths and (optionally) values. Structure-aware, unlike `fs grep`.

## Motivation

LLMs often need to answer "where is X used in this config?" — e.g., "find every place 'localhost' appears", "find every key starting with 'aws_'". Text grep returns false positives (matches in comments, in keys when you wanted values, etc.); a structural search returns dot-paths so the LLM can navigate from the answer.

## Scope

- `--key <regex>` matches against keys
- `--value <regex>` matches against scalar values (strings, numbers, bools)
- Default behavior with no flag: match keys
- Output: `path<TAB>value` lines, sorted by path
- `--paths-only` to suppress values
- Standard flags: `--format`, `--limit`

## Relationship to existing work

A `json grep` equivalent already exists as **sak-llm-2us**. Both should be implemented together since the walking logic belongs in `src/value.rs`.

## Implementation notes

- Add `value::grep(value, key_re, value_re) -> Vec<(String, &Value)>` walker
- Inline tests across all three formats with both key and value patterns

title: Add git domain enhancements: ref-resolve, merge-base, changed-files, shortlog, ls-files, cat-file
description:
Follow-up enhancements for the git domain after the core commands (status, diff, log, show, blame, branch, tags, stash-list, remote, contributors) are implemented.

## Moderate Value

### sak git ref-resolve <ref>
Resolve a ref (branch, tag, HEAD~3) to a commit SHA + metadata.
- Quick lookup without full show output
- Uses repo.revparse_single(ref) -> peel to commit
- Output: <sha> <type> <summary>

### sak git merge-base <ref1> <ref2>
Find common ancestor of two refs.
- Uses repo.merge_base(oid1, oid2)
- Useful for LLMs reasoning about branch relationships
- Output: <sha> <summary>

### sak git changed-files <ref1> [ref2]
List files changed between two refs (simpler than diff --name-only).
- Default ref2 is HEAD
- Uses diff_tree_to_tree() and iterates deltas
- Output: <status>\t<path> (A/M/D/R)
- Sorted by path

## Lower Value (covered by combinations of existing commands)

### sak git shortlog
Author summary with commit counts.
- Derivable from log output but convenient as a dedicated command
- Similar to contributors but grouped differently

### sak git ls-files
List tracked files.
- Derivable from status + fs glob but convenient for 'what does git track?'
- Uses repo.index() -> iterate entries

### sak git cat-file <ref>
Raw object access (blob, tree, commit, tag content).
- Show mostly covers commit use cases
- Useful for inspecting blobs at specific revisions

## Implementation Notes

- All commands follow the same pattern as core git commands
- Use git2 crate APIs (no shelling out)
- Output through BoundedWriter with --limit support
- All operations strictly read-only

title: json head/tail/slice: take first/last N or a slice of an array
description: Add `sak json head <n> [path] [files...]`, `sak json tail <n> [path] [files...]`, and/or `sak json slice <start> <end> [path] [files...]` for arrays. Useful for sampling large arrays without loading the full result through query. Could also be implemented as flags on query.

title: feat(k8s): describe command
description:
## Context

The "one command to look at a broken thing" command. Pulls together everything an LLM needs to understand why a single resource is in the state it is, in one call.

**Not kubectl describe.** kubectl describe is human-prose oriented (paragraphs, blank lines, mixed indentation) and is hostile to LLM parsing. `sak k8s describe` outputs structured tab-separated sections with section headers, the same way `sak git log` and `sak fs grep` already do.

## Command

### `sak k8s describe <kind> <name>`

Args:
- `<kind>`, `<name>` — resolved via `discovery::resolve` (same as `get`)
- `-n, --namespace <ns>`
- `--limit <n>`

Output sections, each prefixed with a `# Section` header line written as a `BoundedWriter` decoration (does not count toward `--limit`):

1. `# Object` — `kind/name`, namespace, age, labels (sorted), annotations (sorted, one per line)
2. `# Status` — top-level `status.phase` / `status.conditions[*]` as `type<TAB>status<TAB>reason<TAB>message`
3. `# Containers` — for each container in the resolved pod-template (using the existing `containers::walk_containers`): `name<TAB>image<TAB>ready<TAB>restarts<TAB>last-state<TAB>last-reason`
4. `# Owners` — walk `metadata.ownerReferences` recursively up to the root: `kind/name` per ancestor
5. `# Events` — last 10 events whose `involvedObject` matches this resource, formatted the same way as `sak k8s events`

## Implementation notes

- Resource fetch: `client::get_dyn`. 404 → exit 1.
- Container statuses live on `status.containerStatuses` (Pod) — for non-Pod kinds we walk `containers::walk_containers` for declared containers and skip the `restarts/last-state/last-reason` columns (or render them as `-`).
- Owner walk is bounded: cap at 5 hops to defend against pathological loops.
- Events fetch shares the same Event-list logic as `sak k8s events --for kind/name`. **Depends on sak k8s events landing first** so the events helper can be factored into a small shared module (or duplicate the helper if events is not yet in flight — flag in the PR description either way).
- All section headers via `BoundedWriter::write_decoration` so they do not get truncated mid-section.

## Files

**Create:**
- `src/k8s/describe.rs`

**Modify:**
- `src/k8s/mod.rs` — add `Describe` variant.
- (Possibly) `src/k8s/events.rs` — extract the events-for-object fetch into a `pub fn events_for(client, ar, namespace, name) -> ...` so describe can call it.

## Dependencies

Depends on `sak k8s events` (separate issue) for the events fetch helper. Can be implemented in parallel up to that point, but the final version should reuse the shared helper rather than duplicating it.

## Acceptance criteria

- `cargo test --features k8s` passes.
- Chokepoint grep test still passes.
- `cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets` clean.
- Manual: `cargo run -- k8s describe deployment <name> -n <ns>` shows all five sections, with events at the bottom.
- Patch-level version bump.

title: feat(sqlite): count command
description:
## Context

Row count for a table, with an optional WHERE clause. The "how big is this" command. Small but worth its own command so LLMs do not have to construct \`SELECT COUNT(*)\` themselves and risk picking up a \`sqlite query\` typo.

Depends on the sqlite domain foundation issue.

## Command

### \`sak sqlite count <db> <table>\`

Args:
- \`<db>\` — path to the SQLite database file
- \`<table>\` — table name
- \`--where <expr>\` — optional SQL boolean expression (no leading \`WHERE\`)

Source:

\`\`\`sql
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM "<table>"
\`\`\`

or, with \`--where\`:

\`\`\`sql
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM "<table>" WHERE <expr>
\`\`\`

## Validation

- Quote the table name by wrapping in double quotes and doubling any internal double quotes.
- Reject \`--where\` containing \`;\` (defense in depth — the connection is read-only and \`query_only=ON\`, but a stray \`;\` indicates the user is trying to chain statements and should get a clear error).
- Reject \`<table>\` containing a NUL byte.

## Output

A single line with the count. Through \`BoundedWriter\`.

## Exit codes

- 0 if the query succeeded (any non-negative count, including 0 — \`count\` is a metadata command, an empty table is a valid answer)
- 2 on error (table missing, malformed where clause, etc.)

Note: \`count\` deviates from the usual sak convention (1 = no results) because the count itself is the result, and zero is meaningful.

## Implementation notes

- Open via \`client::open_readonly\`, run via the existing query helper from the foundation issue.
- Pure \`u64\` extraction from a one-row, one-column result.

## Files

**Create:**
- \`src/sqlite/count.rs\`

**Modify:**
- \`src/sqlite/mod.rs\` — add \`Count\` variant and dispatch.

## Acceptance

- \`cargo test --features sqlite\` passes — unit tests over a temp database covering: empty table → \`0\`; non-empty table; \`--where\` filter; missing table → exit 2; \`--where\` with \`;\` → exit 2 with clear error; table name containing a quoted-double-quote.
- Chokepoint grep test still passes.
- \`cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets\` clean.
- Manual: \`cargo run --features sqlite -- sqlite count some.db users --where "active = 1"\`.
- Patch-level version bump.

title: json schema: infer structural schema from a document
description: Add `sak json schema [files...]` that walks a JSON document and emits an inferred schema -- key names + types, recursively. Output could be JSON Schema or a simpler 'key: type' tree (decide during design). LLMs constantly need to understand shape before writing a query, and currently waste tokens reading whole files. Highest-value addition to the json domain.
close_reason: Implemented sak json schema. Walks a JSON document and emits flat dot-path lines of form 'path: type'. Arrays use [] in the path; element schemas are merged into unions when heterogeneous (e.g. '[]: boolean|number|string'). Root type recorded as '(root): <type>'. Supports --depth to cap recursion. Uses BTreeMap+BTreeSet for sorted, deduplicated output. Reuses read_json_inputs() and type_name() from json/mod.rs. Version bumped to 0.3.1.

title: json paths: list all leaf paths in dot notation
description: Add `sak json paths [files...]` that lists all leaf paths in a JSON document in dot notation. Like `flatten` but values omitted. Pairs with `query`: discover paths, then extract. Options: --max-depth, --separator. Sorted output. Reuses the recursive walker from flatten.rs.

title: json uniq: distinct values at a path across an array
description: Add `sak json uniq <path> [files...]` that emits distinct values at a path across all elements of an array. Like `sort | uniq` but for JSON. Sorted output for determinism.

title: json select: project a subset of fields
description: Add `sak json select <paths...> [files...]` that projects a subset of fields from a JSON object, like SQL SELECT. Output a new object with just the requested keys. Supports nested paths via dot notation. Composes well with `query` (query first, then select fields from the result).

title: Add git domain with status, diff, log, show, blame, branch commands
description:
Add a new `git` domain to sak following the same pattern as the `fs` domain.

## Commands

### `sak git status`
Show working tree status — staged, unstaged, and untracked files.
Wraps `git status --porcelain=v2` for machine-parseable output.

### `sak git diff`
Show file diffs. Supports staged vs unstaged, commit ranges, and path filtering.
Wraps `git diff` with options for `--staged`, `--name-only`, commit refs, and path specs.

### `sak git log`
View commit history with flexible formatting.
Wraps `git log` with options for `--oneline`, `-n` count, `--since`/`--until`, `--author`, `--grep`, path filtering, and `--limit` (via BoundedWriter).

### `sak git show`
Display a specific commit's metadata and diff.
Wraps `git show <ref>` with options for `--stat`, `--name-only`, and `--format`.

### `sak git blame`
Show line-by-line authorship for a file.
Wraps `git blame --porcelain` with options for line ranges (`-L`), `--since`, and formatted output with right-aligned line numbers (reuse `format_line_number()`).

### `sak git branch`
List branches and show current branch.
Wraps `git branch` with options for `--all`, `--remote`, `--sort`, and `--contains`.

## Implementation Notes

- Follow `src/fs/mod.rs` dispatch pattern: `GitCommand` enum + `run()` dispatcher
- Each command in its own file under `src/git/`
- All operations are strictly read-only — no checkout, no commit, no push
- Use `std::process::Command` to shell out to `git`
- Output through `BoundedWriter` with `--limit` support
- Reuse `output.rs` utilities (`format_line_number`, `relative_path`)
- Respect exit code conventions: 0 = results, 1 = no results, 2 = error
- Errors to stderr prefixed with `sak: error:`
- Deterministic/sorted output where applicable
close_reason: Implemented git domain with 10 commands (status, diff, log, show, blame, branch, tags, stash-list, remote, contributors) using git2 crate. All 69 tests pass, clippy clean.

title: json diff: structural diff of two JSON documents
description: Add `sak json diff <a.json> <b.json>` that performs a structural diff of two JSON documents and reports added, removed, and changed paths. Output format: '+ path<TAB>value', '- path<TAB>value', '~ path<TAB>old -> new'. Read-only, naturally fits the sak model. Useful for comparing config files or API responses.

title: Add config merge command (preview a layered merge)
description:
Add `sak config merge <base> <override...>` — show the result of layering one or more override configs onto a base config, written to stdout. Read-only because no files are modified.

## Motivation

Configs are commonly layered (base + environment override + local override). When debugging "what does this config actually look like in production?", an LLM needs to apply the layering manually, which is error-prone. A `merge` command makes the result inspectable in one call.

## Scope and open questions

This is the riskiest command on the list because merge semantics are opinionated. Decisions to make before implementing:

- **Deep merge vs shallow override?** Deep merge is more useful but harder to predict.
- **Array semantics**: replace (last wins) or append? This is the biggest fork — different ecosystems disagree.
- **Type mismatches**: error, or override wins?
- **Output format**: same as the base file's format, or settable via `--format`?

## Suggested defaults

- Deep merge for objects (later overrides win at leaves)
- Replace for arrays (avoids surprises; users who want concat can ask for `--arrays append`)
- Error on type mismatch by default; `--lenient` to let later wins
- Output format defaults to base file's format; `--format` overrides

## Implementation notes

- New `value::merge(base, overrides, opts) -> Value` helper in `src/value.rs`
- Reuse `read_config_inputs` for input
- Reuse the converter from sak-llm-hwc (config convert) for the output side
- Inline tests covering all the open-question scenarios above

## Lower priority because

The semantics are opinionated and risk shipping something users find surprising. Worth doing only after `convert` and `diff` land, since both are dependencies (output formatting reuses convert; verifying merge correctness benefits from diff).

title: feat(k8s): owners and children commands
description:
## Context

Two commands that walk `metadata.ownerReferences` to resolve provenance ("where does this pod come from") and inverse provenance ("what does this Deployment own"). LLMs constantly chain three or four `get` calls to figure this out today; one command per direction is the obvious shortcut.

## Commands

### `sak k8s owners <kind> <name>`

Walks `metadata.ownerReferences` *up* to the root, one hop at a time.

Args:
- `<kind>`, `<name>`
- `-n, --namespace <ns>`
- `--limit <n>`

Output: one line per ancestor, in order from immediate parent to root: `kind/name`. Stops at the first resource with no `ownerReferences`. Cap recursion depth at 8 hops to defend against pathological loops.

Empty output (no owners at all) → exit 1.

### `sak k8s children <kind> <name>`

Walks one level *down*: finds resources whose `ownerReferences` include this object.

Args: same as `owners`.

Output: one line per immediate child: `kind/name`, sorted by `(kind, name)`. Only one level deep — descending recursively explodes badly on Deployments with many ReplicaSet revisions.

Implementation maps each parent kind to its likely-children kinds (the `OWNERSHIP_MAP` table below), lists each child kind in the same namespace, and filters by `ownerReferences[].uid == parent.metadata.uid`. The likely-children list is hardcoded:

| Parent kind   | Child kinds searched              |
|---------------|-----------------------------------|
| Deployment    | ReplicaSet                        |
| StatefulSet   | Pod                               |
| DaemonSet     | Pod                               |
| ReplicaSet    | Pod                               |
| Job           | Pod                               |
| CronJob       | Job                               |
| Service       | EndpointSlice                     |

Anything else → "no known child kinds for {kind}" error, exit 2.

## Implementation notes

- Both commands fetch the parent resource via `client::get_dyn` first to read its `metadata.uid` (children) or `metadata.ownerReferences` (owners). 404 on the parent → exit 1.
- `owners` only needs `get_dyn` calls — one per hop. Cheap.
- `children` does one `list_dyn` call per child kind in the table, then filters in-memory. The table is small enough (max ~2 kinds per parent) to keep this bounded.
- Owner walk uses `metadata.ownerReferences[*].kind` + `.name` to construct the next `get_dyn` call. The `apiVersion` field on the owner reference is used to resolve the right `ApiResource` via `discovery::lookup_builtin` (fast path) or `discovery::resolve` (slow path) — the kind alone is ambiguous if you happen to have a CRD with the same name.

## Files

**Create:**
- `src/k8s/owners.rs`
- `src/k8s/children.rs`

**Modify:**
- `src/k8s/mod.rs` — add `Owners` and `Children` variants.

## Acceptance criteria

- `cargo test --features k8s` passes.
- Chokepoint grep test still passes.
- `cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets` clean.
- Manual:
  - `cargo run -- k8s owners pod <some-pod> -n <ns>` — should walk Pod → ReplicaSet → Deployment.
  - `cargo run -- k8s children deployment <some-deploy> -n <ns>` — should list ReplicaSets.
  - `cargo run -- k8s children pod <some-pod> -n <ns>` — should error cleanly.
- Patch-level version bump.

title: Add config paths command
description:
Add `sak config paths [files...]` — list every leaf path in a config document, sorted, without values. Output is one path per line.

## Motivation

`flatten` already produces `path<TAB>value` lines, so `sak config flatten file.toml | cut -f1` gets you most of the way. A dedicated `paths` command is more discoverable and avoids the pipeline. Useful when an LLM only needs the structure (e.g., to write code that consumes the config) and not the values.

## Scope

- Same flags as `flatten` for consistency: `--separator`, `--max-depth`, `--arrays`, `--format`, `--limit`
- Output sorted lexicographically

## Relationship to existing work

A `json paths` equivalent already exists as **sak-llm-6tn**. Since `value::flatten_value` is shared, both domains should be implemented together — possibly as a flag on `flatten` (e.g., `flatten --paths-only`) rather than a separate command. Worth deciding before implementing either issue.

## Open question

Should this exist as a separate command or just a flag on `flatten`? A flag is simpler but less discoverable in `--help`. Lean toward separate command for LLM discoverability.

## Implementation notes

- Trivially small — reuse `flatten_value` and discard the value column
- Or add `--paths-only` flag to existing `flatten` command and close this in favor of that

title: feat(k8s): can-i command
description:
## Context

Wraps `SelfSubjectAccessReview` — the Kubernetes RBAC self-check API. Equivalent to `kubectl auth can-i`. Critical when an LLM hits a `Forbidden` and needs to know whether to give up, switch context, or escalate to a human.

This command is **read-only with respect to cluster state**, even though the underlying API call is technically a `POST` (`create` on the `SelfSubjectAccessReview` virtual resource). RBAC self-checks are designed to be safe: they check permissions and return a verdict, never persisting anything.

## Command

### `sak k8s can-i <verb> <resource>`

Args:
- `<verb>` — e.g. `get`, `list`, `create`, `delete`, `patch`, `*`
- `<resource>` — e.g. `pods`, `deployments.apps`, `secrets`, `nodes`
- `-n, --namespace <ns>` — optional; omit for cluster-scoped check
- `--subresource <sub>` — e.g. `log`, `status`, `scale`
- `--name <name>` — check on a specific resource name (some authorizers care)

Output: `yes` or `no` on a single line. If denied, also write `reason: <reason>` to stderr (if the apiserver provides one).

Exit codes: 0 if allowed, 1 if denied, 2 on error contacting the apiserver.

## Implementation notes

- The grep test in `client.rs` currently forbids `.create(` on any kube `Api` outside `client.rs`. SelfSubjectAccessReview is the one legitimate read-only "create": the resource is virtual and never persisted. Two implementation options:
  1. Add a dedicated `pub async fn can_i(client, verb, resource_attrs) -> Result<bool>` helper to `client.rs` that wraps the typed `Api::<SelfSubjectAccessReview>::create` call. The helper lives only in `client.rs`, so the grep test still passes for everything else.
  2. Hand-build the POST via the existing `client::request_text` chokepoint (which would need to grow POST support). Cleaner separation but more code.

  **Recommended: option 1.** Add `can_i` to `client.rs` next to `pod_logs` (whenever that lands). Document that `SelfSubjectAccessReview` is the *only* legitimate use of `Api::create` in the entire domain, and that the helper is the chokepoint that contains it.
- The check needs `authorization.k8s.io/v1/SelfSubjectAccessReview` to be available, which it is on every modern apiserver — no graceful-degradation path needed.
- Keep the response shape simple: read `status.allowed` (bool) and `status.reason` (optional string).

## Files

**Create:**
- `src/k8s/can_i.rs`

**Modify:**
- `src/k8s/client.rs` — add `can_i` helper and a small comment block explaining why the `.create(` mention is allowed here. Update the grep test forbidden-token list if necessary so the comment itself does not trip the test (the test already skips comment lines).
- `src/k8s/mod.rs` — add `CanI` variant and dispatch.

## Acceptance criteria

- `cargo test --features k8s` passes (the chokepoint grep test must still pass — `.create(` only appears in `client.rs`).
- `cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets` clean.
- Manual:
  - `cargo run -- k8s can-i list pods -n default` — typically `yes`, exit 0.
  - `cargo run -- k8s can-i delete nodes` — typically `no`, exit 1.
  - `cargo run -- k8s can-i get pods/log -n default` — subresource check.
- Patch-level version bump.

title: json size: report size statistics for a document
description: Add `sak json size [files...]` that reports size statistics for a JSON document: byte size, max nesting depth, total leaf count, max array length, total object key count. Quick 'is this file too big to inline?' check before an LLM tries to read the whole thing.

title: json minify/pretty: reformat JSON output
description: Add `sak json minify [files...]` and `sak json pretty [files...]` (or a single `sak json format` command with flags) for reformatting JSON. Trivial to implement but useful for piping. Alternative: extend query with a no-op default expression so `sak json query . --pretty` works. Lower priority since query --pretty already covers most uses.

title: Add line range exclusion to fs read (--exclude / --invert)
description:
Add the ability to emit a file with one or more line ranges removed -- the inverse of `fs read -n X-Y`.

## Motivation

Came up while editing README.md: needed to remove lines 75-357 from a file. Had to shell out to `awk 'NR<=74 || NR>=358'`. SAK should cover this case directly so LLMs don't reach for awk/sed.

This is read-only and stays within the existing "read a file, emit some of it" model -- the caller redirects stdout to write the result.

## Proposed Design

Extend `sak fs read` with one of:

- `--exclude <ranges>` -- drop the given line ranges, emit everything else
- `--invert` -- invert the meaning of `-n` (emit lines *outside* the specified range)

`--exclude` is more flexible (accepts its own ranges, composes with `-n`). `--invert` is simpler but only works with `-n`.

Recommendation: `--exclude N-M` (with the same range syntax as `-n`), supporting comma-separated multiple ranges like `--exclude 1-10,50-60`.

## Examples

\`\`\`
# Drop lines 75-357 from README.md
sak fs read README.md --exclude 75-357 > /tmp/new && mv /tmp/new README.md

# Drop multiple ranges
sak fs read data.txt --exclude 1-10,90-100
\`\`\`

## Implementation Notes

- Reuse the existing range parser used by `-n`
- `--exclude` and `-n` should be combinable (read range A, then drop sub-ranges)
- Line numbers in output should still reflect original file positions when `--line-numbers` is on
- Add tests covering single range, multiple ranges, and combination with `-n`

title: feat(k8s): events command
description:
## Context

Second-most-used debug command after `logs`. When something is broken, the first question is always "what does the apiserver have to say about it" — and the answer lives in the `events` stream. `kubectl get events --sort-by=.lastTimestamp` is what every SRE has aliased; sak should ship that out of the box, plus an `--for kind/name` filter so the LLM can ask "events about *this* object" without piping through `sak fs grep`.

## Command

### `sak k8s events`

Args:
- `-n, --namespace <ns>` / `-A, --all-namespaces` (mutually exclusive — same shape as `get`)
- `--for <kind/name>` — filter to events whose `involvedObject` matches this kind/name. Kind is resolved through `discovery::lookup_builtin` (no apiserver call) for canonicalization.
- `--limit <n>`

Output: `last<TAB>type<TAB>reason<TAB>kind/name<TAB>message`, sorted by `lastTimestamp` *descending* (newest first — opposite of sak default, but events are time-series and the LLM almost always wants the most recent first). If `lastTimestamp` is null, fall back to `eventTime`, then `firstTimestamp`.

Exit codes per sak convention: 0 found, 1 none, 2 error.

## Implementation notes

- Events live at `core/v1/Event`. Use the existing `list_dyn` chokepoint with the resolved `ApiResource`.
- `--for` is applied client-side after fetching: filter on `involvedObject.kind` (case-insensitive) and `involvedObject.name` (exact). Keep it client-side because field selectors on Event nesting are unreliable across apiserver versions.
- Multi-line `message` fields should be collapsed to a single line (replace `\n` with ` `) so each event stays one row.
- Timestamps: emit in RFC3339 in UTC for determinism. Do not localize.
- All output through `BoundedWriter`.

## Files

**Create:**
- `src/k8s/events.rs`

**Modify:**
- `src/k8s/mod.rs` — add `Events` variant and dispatch.

## Acceptance criteria

- `cargo test --features k8s` passes (add unit tests for the timestamp-fallback ordering on hand-built fixtures).
- Chokepoint grep test still passes.
- `cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets` clean.
- Manual:
  - `cargo run -- k8s events -A --limit 20` — recent events cluster-wide.
  - `cargo run -- k8s events --for pod/some-pod -n some-ns` — events for one object.
- Patch-level version bump.

title: Add config count command
description:
Add `sak config count <path> [files...]` — return the count of items at a path: array length for arrays, key count for objects, character count for strings, 1 for scalars, 0 for null.

## Motivation

LLMs often want to know "how many X are in this config?" before deciding how to process it. Doing this with `query` plus shell tools is awkward (need to parse JSON output, count elements). A dedicated command is cleaner.

## Output

A single integer on stdout. Exit code 0 if the path exists, 1 if missing.

## Relationship to existing work

Two json equivalents exist as separate issues: **sak-llm-keo** (`json length`) and **sak-llm-ym6** (`json count`). The naming overlap suggests these might want to be unified before implementing — `length` is cleaner for "how big is this thing?" while `count` more naturally suggests counting matches of a predicate. Worth resolving the json domain naming first, then adopting the same name in `config`.

## Implementation notes

- Once the json equivalent lands, the config version is a near-copy with `read_json_inputs` swapped for `read_config_inputs`
- The counting helper itself should live in `src/value.rs` so both domains share it

title: json type: print value type at a path
description: Add `sak json type [path] [files...]` that prints the JSON type at a given path (object, array, string, number, boolean, null). Cheap discovery without dumping the value. Reuses resolve_expression() in src/json/mod.rs and type_name(). Reads stdin when no files.

title: Add json exists command
description:
Add `sak json exists <path> [files...]` — boolean check whether a path exists in a JSON document. Exit code only (0 = exists, 1 = missing); produces no stdout output.

## Motivation

Exit-code-driven commands are valuable in agent loops and shell pipelines:

```sh
sak json exists .features.beta config.json && enable_beta_path
```

`query` already returns exit code 1 for missing paths, but it also writes the value to stdout when found, which is wasted output for a pure existence check. A dedicated `exists` command makes the intent clear in `--help` and avoids the spurious output.

## Scope

- Same path syntax as `query` (dot notation + JSON Pointer, via `value::resolve_expression`)
- Reads from stdin if no files are given
- Multiple files: exit 0 if **all** files contain the path; exit 1 if any are missing (or `--any` flag to flip the semantics?)
- No stdout output by default; `--verbose` could echo the value if found

## Relationship to other domains

A sibling `config exists` issue exists as **sak-llm-ozn**. Both should be implemented together since they both call `value::resolve_expression` and the only difference is the input reader (`read_json_inputs` vs `read_config_inputs`).

## Implementation notes

- Trivially small — `src/json/exists.rs` calls `resolve_expression` and returns the appropriate ExitCode
- Inline tests for present/absent paths, root path, JSON Pointer syntax

title: feat(sqlite): tables command
description:
## Context

First read command in the new \`sqlite\` domain. Lists tables and views in a SQLite database. The "what is in this file" answer that every LLM session asking about a \`.db\` file starts with.

Depends on the sqlite domain foundation issue.

## Command

### \`sak sqlite tables <db>\`

Args:
- \`<db>\` — path to the SQLite database file
- \`--views\` — also include views (off by default — listing only tables is the common case)
- \`--system\` — include internal \`sqlite_*\` tables (off by default)
- \`--limit <n>\`

Source query (when neither \`--views\` nor \`--system\` is set):

\`\`\`sql
SELECT name, type FROM sqlite_master
WHERE type = 'table' AND name NOT LIKE 'sqlite_%'
ORDER BY name
\`\`\`

With \`--views\`: include \`type = 'view'\`. With \`--system\`: drop the \`NOT LIKE 'sqlite_%'\` clause.

Output: \`name<TAB>type\`, sorted by name. Through \`BoundedWriter\`.

Exit codes: 0 if any rows, 1 if zero rows, 2 on error.

## Implementation notes

- Open the database via \`client::open_readonly\` from the foundation issue.
- Run the query through \`client::query_rows\` (do **not** import \`rusqlite::Connection\` directly — chokepoint test will fail).
- All output through \`BoundedWriter\`.

## Files

**Create:**
- \`src/sqlite/tables.rs\`

**Modify:**
- \`src/sqlite/mod.rs\` — add \`Tables\` variant to \`SqliteCommand\` and dispatch.

## Acceptance

- \`cargo test --features sqlite\` passes — add unit tests over a temp database with at least one user table, one view, and one \`sqlite_*\` internal table, exercising the default and \`--views\` / \`--system\` filters.
- Chokepoint grep test still passes.
- \`cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets\` clean.
- Manual: \`cargo run --features sqlite -- sqlite tables some.db\`.
- \`sak sqlite tables --help\` has \`long_about\` and \`after_help\` examples per sak conventions.
- Patch-level version bump.

title: Add NixOS flake for packaging sak
description:
Add a flake.nix so sak is consumable on NixOS systems via flake input.

## Approach

- Use `rustPlatform.buildRustPackage` (standard nixpkgs pattern)
- No flake-utils — use `nixpkgs.lib.genAttrs` directly
- Bundled libgit2 (git2 crate default, no system libgit2 dependency)

## Details

- inputs: nixpkgs-unstable
- outputs: `packages.<system>.default` for x86_64-linux, aarch64-linux, x86_64-darwin, aarch64-darwin
- nativeBuildInputs: pkg-config (needed by git2 bundled build)
- buildInputs: openssl (Linux), Security + SystemConfiguration frameworks (macOS)
- cargoLock.lockFile = ./Cargo.lock
- Source filtering: exclude .git, target, .beads from Nix store copy
- Add `result` to .gitignore (Nix build symlink)
- Install-only — no devShell needed

## Consumer usage

```nix
# In a NixOS or home-manager flake:
inputs.sak.url = "github:patsoffice/sak";
environment.systemPackages = [ inputs.sak.packages.${system}.default ];
```

## Verification

```bash
nix build
./result/bin/sak --version
nix flake check
```
close_reason: Added flake.nix with rustPlatform.buildRustPackage, bundled libgit2, multi-platform support. Builds and passes nix flake check.

title: feat(k8s): contexts command
description:
## Context

Safety-first command. An LLM that does not know which cluster it is pointed at is the most dangerous thing in the entire k8s domain. `sak k8s contexts` lets the LLM see — and the user verify — which kubeconfig context is active before any read against the cluster. Should be the first command an LLM runs in a fresh k8s session.

This is the cheapest possible k8s command: it never contacts any apiserver. It only parses kubeconfig.

## Command

### `sak k8s contexts`

No required args. Lists every context in the merged kubeconfig, marks the current one, and exits.

Args:
- `--limit <n>`

Output: `current<TAB>name<TAB>cluster<TAB>user<TAB>namespace`, one line per context, sorted by name. The `current` column is `*` for the active context, empty otherwise. Goes through `BoundedWriter`.

Exit codes: 0 if any contexts found, 1 if the kubeconfig has none, 2 on parse error.

## Implementation notes

- Use `kube::config::Kubeconfig::read()` (or `read_from()` if `KUBECONFIG` is a colon-list) to merge the kubeconfig files exactly the way `kube::Client::try_default` does. Do **not** call `Client::try_default()` — that opens a real connection. We just want the parsed config.
- The current context comes from the loaded `Kubeconfig.current_context` field.
- Namespace per context is `context.namespace`, defaulting to empty (clients map empty → `default` at request time, but we should report what kubeconfig literally says).
- This command does not import `kube::Api` and does not need any chokepoint extension. The grep test still passes trivially.

## Files

**Create:**
- `src/k8s/contexts.rs`

**Modify:**
- `src/k8s/mod.rs` — add `Contexts` variant and dispatch.

## Acceptance criteria

- `cargo test --features k8s` passes.
- `cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets` clean.
- Manual: `cargo run -- k8s contexts` against a kubeconfig with multiple entries shows the right `*` marker on the active one.
- `sak k8s contexts --help` has `long_about` and `after_help` examples per sak conventions.
- Patch-level version bump.

title: Add GitHub CI workflow for build, test, clippy, and fmt
description:
Add a GitHub Actions CI workflow that runs on push and PR to main.

## Checks

- `cargo fmt --check` — formatting
- `cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets` — lint (must be warning-free)
- `cargo test` — all tests pass
- `cargo build --release` — release build succeeds

## Notes

- Use a single workflow file: `.github/workflows/ci.yml`
- Target stable Rust toolchain
- Cache cargo registry and build artifacts for speed
- Run fmt and clippy as separate jobs so failures are clearly attributable
- Consider matrix for multiple OS (Linux at minimum, macOS nice-to-have)
- git2 crate requires libgit2 system deps — ensure CI installs them or uses the bundled feature
close_reason: GitHub Actions CI workflow added in .github/workflows/ci.yml with fmt, clippy, test, release build, and nix-build matrix jobs (Linux + macOS). Nix builds push to patsoffice Cachix cache so users can pull pre-built binaries.

title: Add config convert command
description:
Add `sak config convert --to <format> [files...]` to translate between TOML, YAML, plist, and JSON. Source files are not touched — output goes to stdout — so this stays within the read-only contract.

## Motivation

Agents frequently need to feed a config to a tool that wants a different format than the source (e.g., reading a binary plist as JSON to inspect it, or converting a YAML manifest to TOML for a Rust tool). Doing this externally is annoying because each pair has different tooling. Centralizing it lets LLMs say "give me this as YAML" without leaving sak.

## Scope

- Source formats: TOML, YAML, plist (XML or binary), JSON
- Target formats: TOML, YAML, plist (XML), JSON
- `--from` flag for explicit source format (required for stdin); auto-detect by extension otherwise
- `--to` flag is required and accepts toml|yaml|plist|json
- `--pretty` flag for JSON/YAML pretty-printing (default depends on target)

## Implementation notes

- All formats round-trip through `serde_json::Value`
- Reuse `read_config_inputs` for input
- For JSON output: `serde_json::to_string[_pretty]`
- For TOML output: `toml::to_string[_pretty]`
- For YAML output: `serde_yaml::to_string`
- For plist output: `plist::to_writer_xml` (binary plist output is probably out of scope — XML is the universal target)
- Document the lossy edges in `--long_about`: TOML datetimes round-trip as strings, plist Data fields lose their type, etc.

## Open questions

- Where does this command live? It's logically a `config` operation but JSON is also a target. Probably keep it under `config` since the matrix of formats is wider there; the `json` domain stays focused on json-specific operations.

title: feat(k8s): schema command
description:
## Context

Fourth and last issue adding the `k8s` domain. Adds a single command, `sak k8s schema <kind>`, that fetches the OpenAPI v3 schema for a kind from the connected cluster and prints it as JSON.

This is the smallest and most independent of the four issues — it does **not** use the dynamic resource pipeline (\`Api<DynamicObject>\` / \`discovery.rs\`) at all. It uses the \`request_text\` chokepoint helper added in the foundation issue. Cleanly isolated, good \"spare cycles\" issue.

**Depends on sak-llm-k7e (foundation).** Can be developed in parallel with sak-llm-ovb and sak-llm-m78.

## Command

### \`sak k8s schema <kind>\`

Args:
- \`<kind>\` — e.g. \`Deployment\`, \`Pod\`, \`Service\`, or fully qualified \`apps/v1/Deployment\`
- \`--limit\`
- (optional) \`--group <g>\` and \`--version <v>\` if the user needs to disambiguate kinds that exist in multiple groups (e.g. \`Ingress\` in \`networking.k8s.io\` vs the deprecated \`extensions\`)

Output: pretty JSON of the schema for that kind, through \`BoundedWriter\`.

## Implementation

The Kubernetes OpenAPI v3 endpoint is a **two-hop** fetch:

1. \`GET /openapi/v3\` — returns an index of paths, e.g. \`{ \"paths\": { \"apis/apps/v1\": { \"serverRelativeURL\": \"/openapi/v3/apis/apps/v1?hash=...\" }, ... } }\`
2. Resolve the user's kind to a \`(group, version)\` pair (use the same shortname/builtin map from \`discovery.rs\`, or accept fully qualified input).
3. \`GET /openapi/v3/apis/<group>/<version>\` (using the \`serverRelativeURL\` from step 1 to pick up the hash query string).
4. Parse the returned OpenAPI document, find the schema for the requested kind under \`components.schemas\`, and print it.

Both fetches go through \`client::request_text\` from the foundation chokepoint.

**No fallback to \`/openapi/v2\`.** Older clusters that only expose v2 get a clear error. Keeping the implementation small.

## Files

**Create:**
- \`src/k8s/schema.rs\`

**Modify:**
- \`src/k8s/mod.rs\` — add \`Schema\` variant, wire dispatch.
- (Possibly) \`src/k8s/discovery.rs\` — if the kind→(group, version) mapping needs to be reused, expose it as a public helper. The shortname map already has this info; just add an accessor.

## Acceptance criteria

- \`cargo test --features k8s\` passes.
- Chokepoint grep test still passes (\`schema.rs\` must use \`client::request_text\`, not \`kube::Api\` directly).
- \`cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets\` clean.
- Manual integration tests against a real cluster:
  - \`cargo run --features k8s -- k8s schema Deployment | sak json query .properties.spec.type\`
  - \`cargo run --features k8s -- k8s schema Pod | sak json keys . --depth 2\`
  - \`cargo run --features k8s -- k8s schema NotARealKind\` → clear error
- Patch-level version bump.

## Known limitations

- \`/openapi/v2\` not supported. Old clusters error out cleanly.
- Schemas for CRDs work as long as the CRD's group/version is registered in the OpenAPI index, which it is by default in modern k8s.
close_reason:
Landed sak k8s schema as the fourth and final command in the k8s domain. The k8s domain is now complete (kinds, get, images, env, schema).

Implementation:
- src/k8s/schema.rs: two-hop OpenAPI v3 fetch via the chokepoint. Hop 1 GETs /openapi/v3 to find the serverRelativeURL (with hash query string) for the requested group/version. Hop 2 GETs that URL and looks up the schema in components.schemas by walking each entry's x-kubernetes-group-version-kind annotation — the package-style key (io.k8s.api.apps.v1.Deployment) is *not* used for matching, so CRDs and aliased schemas work the same way as builtins.
- Kind resolution is purely client-side: explicit --group/--version (must be supplied together) > fully qualified 'group/version/Kind' or 'version/Kind' > builtin shortname lookup via discovery::lookup_builtin. No cluster contact for resolution itself, so 'sak k8s schema NotARealKind' fails fast with a clear error and never burns a round trip.
- No /openapi/v2 fallback by design — old apiservers fail with a clean error message rather than carrying duplicate schema-walking code.

Chokepoint extension:
- src/k8s/client.rs gained pub async fn request_text(client, path), wrapping kube::Client::request_text. Required adding 'http = { version = "1", optional = true }' to Cargo.toml gated on the k8s feature — kube does not re-export the http::Request types we need to construct raw GETs. The chokepoint grep test still passes (schema.rs only calls client::request_text, never touches kube::Api).

Tests:
- 10 unit tests in schema.rs cover parse_qualified (3-part, 2-part core, plain, empty-segment rejection), openapi_path_key (core api/<v> vs apis/<g>/<v>), and schema_matches_gvk (positive, kind-case-insensitive, wrong-group rejection, missing annotation, multi-tagged array). All pure — no cluster needed.

Verified end-to-end against KUBECONFIG=../home-ops/kubeconfig:
- k8s schema Deployment -> pretty JSON with full descriptions
- ... | sak json query .properties.kind.type -> 'string' (full pipe round-trip)
- ... | sak json keys . --depth 2 -> shows x-kubernetes-group-version-kind, properties, etc.
- k8s schema Ingress --group networking.k8s.io --version v1 -> disambiguation flags work
- k8s schema apps/v1/Deployment -> fully-qualified form works
- k8s schema NotARealKind -> clear error, exit 2

cargo build, cargo build --features k8s, cargo test, cargo test --features k8s, cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets, cargo fmt --check all clean. Cargo.toml bumped 0.5.2 -> 0.5.3 (patch bump for new command within an existing domain).

title: Add toml/yaml domain with query, keys, flatten, validate commands
description:
Add a new `config` domain to sak for reading TOML and YAML files, following the `fs` domain pattern.

## Commands

### `sak config query <expression> [files...]`
Extract values by dot-path from TOML or YAML files.
- Auto-detect format by extension (.toml, .yaml, .yml) or `--format toml|yaml`
- Dot-path syntax: `.server.port`, `.dependencies.serde.version`
- Options: `--raw` (output raw strings without quotes), `--format <toml|yaml>`
- Read from stdin with explicit `--format`

### `sak config keys [path] [files...]`
List keys at a given level or path.
- Default: list top-level keys
- Options: `--depth <n>` (recurse N levels), `--types` (show value types), `--format <toml|yaml>`
- Sorted output for determinism

### `sak config flatten [files...]`
Flatten nested config into `path<TAB>value` lines.
- Same output format as `json flatten` for consistency
- Options: `--separator <char>` (default `.`), `--max-depth <n>`, `--format <toml|yaml>`
- Sorted by path for determinism

### `sak config validate [files...]`
Check whether files contain valid TOML/YAML.
- Auto-detect format by extension
- Exit 0 if valid, exit 1 if invalid
- Report filename, line, column, error message
- Options: `--quiet`, `--format <toml|yaml>`

## Implementation Notes

- Follow `src/fs/mod.rs` dispatch pattern: `ConfigCommand` enum + `run()` dispatcher
- Each command in its own file under `src/config/`
- All operations are strictly read-only
- Parse to a common `serde_json::Value` or custom enum internally so command logic is format-agnostic
- Output through `BoundedWriter` with `--limit` support
- Read from stdin when no files given (requires explicit `--format`)
- Respect exit code conventions: 0 = results, 1 = no results/invalid, 2 = error

## Dependencies

- `toml` crate
- `serde_yaml` or `yaml-rust2` crate
- `serde_json` (shared with json domain for internal representation)
close_reason: Added config domain with query/keys/flatten/validate commands. Supports TOML, YAML, and plist (XML and binary) — plist included beyond original scope because its rich type system maps cleanly to the same internal serde_json::Value representation. INI deliberately excluded as a poor fit for the generic command shape. Shared value-walking utilities extracted to src/value.rs so json and config domains share traversal/path-resolution code.

title: feat(sqlite): dump command
description:
## Context

Sample rows from a table. Friendlier-flag wrapper around \`sak sqlite query\`'s \`SELECT * FROM <table> [ORDER BY ...] LIMIT N\` that handles table-name quoting and gives the LLM ergonomic flags. The "show me what is in this table" command.

Depends on the sqlite domain foundation issue, and shares the type-mapping helper from the \`query\` issue.

## Command

### \`sak sqlite dump <db> <table>\`

Args:
- \`<db>\` — path to the SQLite database file
- \`<table>\` — table or view name
- \`--limit <n>\` — row cap (default 10 — small enough to be safe by default)
- \`--order-by <col>\` — column to order by; sak quotes it
- \`--desc\` — order descending instead of ascending (no-op without \`--order-by\`)
- \`--format <json|tsv>\` — same as \`sak sqlite query\` (default \`json\`)

Effective query (built internally — never exposed to the user as raw SQL):

\`\`\`sql
SELECT * FROM "<table>" [ORDER BY "<col>" [DESC]] LIMIT <n>
\`\`\`

## Validation

- Quote \`<table>\` and \`<col>\` with double-quote identifier escaping (double internal quotes).
- Reject either argument containing a NUL byte.
- \`--limit\` must be a positive integer (clap should enforce).

## Output / type mapping

Reuse the type-mapping helper from the \`sak sqlite query\` command (extracted to a small \`mod\` shared by both files — \`src/sqlite/format.rs\` is fine, or pub-in-crate from \`query.rs\`). Same JSON-lines / TSV behavior, same NULL/INTEGER/REAL/TEXT/BLOB rules, same BoundedWriter output.

## Exit codes

- 0 if at least one row was emitted
- 1 if the table exists but has zero rows (or fewer than the limit AND the limit was respected — same condition: zero rows emitted means exit 1)
- 2 on error (missing table, malformed column, etc.)

## Implementation notes

- Open via \`client::open_readonly\` and run via the same \`query_rows_dyn\` helper from the foundation / query issue.
- This file should be small — most of the work is shared with \`query.rs\`.

## Files

**Create:**
- \`src/sqlite/dump.rs\`

**Modify:**
- \`src/sqlite/mod.rs\` — add \`Dump\` variant and dispatch.
- \`src/sqlite/query.rs\` (or \`src/sqlite/format.rs\`) — make the row → JSON / TSV helpers \`pub(crate)\` so \`dump.rs\` can call them.

## Acceptance

- \`cargo test --features sqlite\` passes — unit tests over a temp database covering: ascending order, descending order, default limit, explicit limit, missing column → exit 2, table with zero rows → exit 1.
- Chokepoint grep test still passes.
- \`cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets\` clean.
- Manual: \`cargo run --features sqlite -- sqlite dump some.db users --limit 5 --order-by id --desc\`.
- Patch-level version bump.

title: feat(k8s): foundation — cargo feature, tokio bridge, kube client chokepoint
description:
## Context

First of four issues adding a `k8s` domain to sak. This issue lands the scaffolding only — no user-facing commands. The point is to absorb the high-risk pieces (dep footprint, build size, async bridge) on their own so they can be reviewed and shaken out before any command code piles on top.

The k8s domain talks read-only to a live Kubernetes cluster via kubeconfig, using the `kube` crate (no shelling out — matches the git domain's `git2` precedent). To preserve sak's small-binary ethos for users who don't need k8s, the **entire domain is gated behind a cargo feature `k8s` that is off by default**. Default builds are unaffected in size and compile time.

## Scope

### Cargo.toml

- Add optional deps: `kube`, `k8s-openapi`, `tokio`
- Add `[features] k8s = ["dep:kube", "dep:k8s-openapi", "dep:tokio"]` (default = [])
- `kube`: `default-features = false`, features `["client", "rustls-tls"]` only. **Do not** enable `runtime`, `derive`, `ws`, `oauth`, `oidc`, `gzip`, `admission`, `jsonpatch` — none are needed for get/list and they significantly inflate build time.
- `k8s-openapi`: pin a single concrete API-version feature (e.g. `v1_31`). **Never** use `latest` — it's a moving target and breaks reproducible builds.
- `tokio`: features `["rt", "macros"]` only. Current-thread runtime is sufficient; do not enable `rt-multi-thread`.
- Verify the latest stable `kube` and matching `k8s-openapi` versions on docs.rs at implementation time before pinning.
- Bump sak's version per CLAUDE.md (minor bump for new domain).

### src/main.rs

```rust
#[cfg(feature = "k8s")]
mod k8s;

#[derive(Subcommand)]
enum Command {
    // ... existing variants ...
    #[cfg(feature = "k8s")]
    #[command(subcommand)]
    K8s(k8s::K8sCommand),
}
```

Add the matching gated dispatch arm in `main()`.

### src/k8s/mod.rs

- `K8sCommand` enum (empty for now — will be populated by issues #2/#3/#4)
- `pub fn run(cmd: &K8sCommand) -> Result<ExitCode>` that builds a current-thread tokio runtime locally and `block_on`s the async dispatch:

```rust
pub fn run(cmd: &K8sCommand) -> Result<ExitCode> {
    let rt = tokio::runtime::Builder::new_current_thread()
        .enable_all()
        .build()?;
    rt.block_on(async { dispatch(cmd).await })
}
```

The rest of sak stays sync.

### src/k8s/client.rs — sole `kube::Api` chokepoint

This module is the **only** file in `src/k8s/` permitted to import `kube::Api` or any of its mutation methods. It exposes a small async surface:

- `pub async fn build_client() -> Result<kube::Client>` — wraps `Client::try_default()`
- `pub async fn list_dyn(client, ar, namespace, list_params) -> Result<ObjectList<DynamicObject>>`
- `pub async fn get_dyn(client, ar, namespace, name) -> Result<DynamicObject>`
- `pub async fn request_text(client, path) -> Result<String>` — for `/openapi/v3` fetches

Read-only enforcement is **convention + a grep test** (kube has no read-only client variant). Add a unit test in `client.rs`:

```rust
#[test]
fn no_mutation_methods_outside_client_module() {
    // Walk src/k8s/*.rs excluding client.rs; assert none contain
    // \".create(\", \".delete(\", \".delete_collection(\", \".patch(\",
    // \".replace(\", \".patch_scale(\", or \"DeleteParams\"/\"PatchParams\".
}
```

### src/k8s/discovery.rs — shortname + GVK resolver

- Hardcoded map of common builtins: `po`/`pod`/`pods` → core/v1/Pod, `deploy`/`deployment`/`deployments` → apps/v1/Deployment, `svc`/`service`/`services` → core/v1/Service, etc. (~30 entries covering the common kinds).
- `pub async fn resolve(client, kind: &str) -> Result<ApiResource>`:
  1. Fast path: lookup in shortname map → call `kube::discovery::oneshot::pinned_kind(&client, &gvk)` (single-GVK, cheap).
  2. Slow path on miss: fall back to full `Discovery::new(client).run().await` and search by name. Document that this is multi-second on big clusters.
- Unit tests on the shortname map (no cluster needed).

## Acceptance criteria

- `cargo build` succeeds and is byte-identical to before this change (default features, k8s off).
- `cargo build --features k8s` succeeds. Record the binary-size delta and cold-build-time delta in the commit message — this is the data point that justifies (or doesn't) the rest of the work.
- `cargo test` passes (default features).
- `cargo test --features k8s` passes (adds the chokepoint grep test + discovery shortname tests).
- `cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets` passes with no warnings.
- `cargo fmt` clean.
- No user-facing command exists yet — `sak k8s --help` (with the feature on) should show an empty subcommand list, that's expected.

## Files

**Modify:**
- \`Cargo.toml\`
- \`src/main.rs\`

**Create:**
- \`src/k8s/mod.rs\`
- \`src/k8s/client.rs\`
- \`src/k8s/discovery.rs\`

## Blocks

This issue blocks issues #2 (kinds + get), #3 (images + env), and #4 (schema). Land this first.

## Known trade-offs

- **Binary size**: \`--features k8s\` builds will be ~15–25 MB larger and ~60–120s slower to cold-build than the default. This is the price of \`k8s-openapi\`'s generated code. Default builds are unaffected.
- **Read-only enforcement is convention + grep test**, not type-level. \`kube::Api\` exposes mutation methods on the same type used for reads. The chokepoint + grep test is the cheapest credible defense.
close_reason: Foundation landed: k8s cargo feature (off by default), tokio current-thread bridge, kube::Api chokepoint in src/k8s/client.rs (with grep test enforcing no mutation methods or Api imports outside that file), and src/k8s/discovery.rs with a 32-entry builtin shortname table + fast/slow path resolver. Default build unchanged; --features k8s adds 7 unit tests (all green) and a #[ignore]d live_smoke test verified end-to-end against a real cluster (build_client + fast-path resolve(pod) + list_dyn 241 pods + slow-path resolve(Lease) -> coordination.k8s.io/v1). cargo build, cargo build --features k8s, cargo test, cargo test --features k8s, cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets, and cargo fmt all clean. Real binary-size delta deferred until sak-llm-ovb wires the first command — LTO strips the dead kube tree in foundation alone.

title: json length: length of array/object/string at a path
description: Add `sak json length [path] [files...]` that prints the length of the value at a path: array element count, object key count, string char count. Errors on scalar types. Essential for 'how many X are there' without reading the whole structure. Reuses resolve_expression().

title: feat(k8s): endpoints command
description:
## Context

`Service has no endpoints` is one of the top three k8s footguns. The current debugging path (`kubectl describe svc`, then `kubectl get endpoints`, then `kubectl get pods -l <selector>`) is awful. A focused command beats all of them.

## Command

### `sak k8s endpoints <service>`

Args:
- `<service>` — service name
- `-n, --namespace <ns>`
- `--limit <n>`

Output: `address<TAB>port<TAB>node<TAB>ready<TAB>target` per backing endpoint, sorted by `(address, port)`.

- `address` — IP from `EndpointSlice.endpoints[*].addresses[0]`
- `port`    — combined as `name=port/protocol` from `EndpointSlice.ports[*]`; one row per `(endpoint × port)` combo
- `node`    — `EndpointSlice.endpoints[*].nodeName` (or `-`)
- `ready`   — `EndpointSlice.endpoints[*].conditions.ready` (`true`/`false`/`unknown`)
- `target`  — `EndpointSlice.endpoints[*].targetRef.kind/name` (or `-`)

## Implementation notes

- Modern clusters (1.21+) use `discovery.k8s.io/v1/EndpointSlice`; the legacy `core/v1/Endpoints` is deprecated. Use EndpointSlice only.
- Discovery: hardcode `discovery.k8s.io/v1/EndpointSlice` (no need to call `discovery::resolve`) — saves a round trip and EndpointSlice is stable enough to inline.
- Filter EndpointSlices by label `kubernetes.io/service-name=<service>`. List via `client::list_dyn` with `ListParams::default().labels(...)`.
- A service can have multiple EndpointSlices (large services are sharded). Walk all of them and emit one row per `(endpoint, port)` combination.
- If zero EndpointSlices come back, exit 1 (the sak "no results" code) — a service with no endpoints *is* the diagnostic we are reporting.

## Files

**Create:**
- `src/k8s/endpoints.rs`

**Modify:**
- `src/k8s/mod.rs` — add `Endpoints` variant.

## Acceptance criteria

- `cargo test --features k8s` passes (unit-test the EndpointSlice → row flattening on a fixture with two endpoints × two ports).
- Chokepoint grep test still passes.
- `cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets` clean.
- Manual:
  - `cargo run -- k8s endpoints kubernetes -n default` — should always have endpoints on every cluster.
  - `cargo run -- k8s endpoints <some-service> -n <ns>`
- Patch-level version bump.

title: Add INI file support to config domain
description:
Extend the `config` domain to handle INI files alongside the existing TOML/YAML/plist formats. INI was deliberately excluded from the initial implementation (sak-llm-iw0) because its flat, untyped structure is a poor fit for the generic command shape, but it remains a real-world format LLMs encounter and is worth supporting at a reduced fidelity.

## Why this is lower priority

Unlike TOML/YAML/plist, INI doesn't fit cleanly into the shared `serde_json::Value` model:

- **No standard spec** — dialects vary across `rust-ini`, `configparser`, and others (comment characters, multiline values, key=value vs key:value, case sensitivity, etc.)
- **Shallow by design** — structure is always exactly two levels: `section.key = value`. Depth-related flags (`keys --depth`, `flatten --max-depth`) become trivial or meaningless.
- **All values are untyped strings** — there's no distinction between `port = 8080` (number) and `name = alice` (string). The `--types` flag on `keys` returns `string` for everything; `schema` would be uniformly `string`.

These mismatches mean INI gets less value out of each command than the typed formats.

## What still works

Even with the caveats, the four core commands have meaningful INI semantics:

- `query .section.key file.ini` — returns the string value (always)
- `keys file.ini` — top-level section names; `keys .section file.ini` — keys within a section
- `flatten file.ini` — `section.key<TAB>value` lines, sorted
- `validate file.ini` — parse-only check; useful because INI files break in subtle ways
- `exists .section.key file.ini` — section/key existence check

## Implementation notes

- Add a Rust INI crate dependency. `rust-ini` (`ini = "1"`) is the most popular and supports the common dialects. Verify it can produce a structure that converts cleanly into `serde_json::Value` (a two-level object: section → key → string-value).
- Add `Format::Ini` to the `Format` enum in `src/config/mod.rs`
- Extend `detect_format()` to recognize `.ini`, `.cfg`, and `.conf` (the latter two are debatable — they're heavily overloaded; consider `.ini` only at first)
- Extend `parse_one()` with an INI branch that parses to `Ini` and converts to `Value::Object<section, Object<key, String>>`
- Update `--long_about` text on each command to call out the INI-specific limitations (everything-is-a-string, depth flags become trivial)
- Add inline tests covering: top-level keys (= section names), section keys, flatten output, validate on a malformed file
- Decide on dialect handling: probably accept the most permissive defaults and document them; do not expose dialect tuning flags initially

## Open questions

- **Comment styles**: `;` and `#` are both common; pick the parser default and document it
- **Sectionless keys**: some INI dialects allow keys before any `[section]` header. Map them to a synthetic top-level key (e.g., `""` or `_global`)? Reject them? The parser's default behavior is the safe call.
- **Duplicate keys**: INI allows them in some dialects. Last wins, first wins, or error? Probably last wins to match merge semantics elsewhere.
- **`.cfg`/`.conf` extensions**: too overloaded to safely auto-detect. Start with `.ini` only and require `--format ini` for the others.

## Background

Was discussed during scoping for sak-llm-iw0 and intentionally deferred. See that issue's close reason for the original rationale.

title: Add csv domain with query, headers, stats, validate commands
description:
Add a new `csv` domain to sak following the same pattern as the `fs` domain.

## Commands

### `sak csv query <columns> [files...]`
Select and filter CSV rows/columns.
- Column selection by name or index: `--columns name,age` or `--columns 1,3`
- Options: `--filter <column>=<value>` (exact match), `--filter-regex <column>=<pattern>`, `--delimiter <char>` (default `,`), `--no-header` (treat first row as data), `--limit`
- Output as CSV with header row
- Read from stdin when no files given

### `sak csv headers [files...]`
List column names and their indices.
- Output: `index<TAB>name` for each column
- Options: `--delimiter <char>`, `--types` (infer column types by sampling rows: string, integer, float, bool, empty)

### `sak csv stats [files...]`
Show summary statistics for CSV columns.
- Row count, column count, null/empty counts per column
- For numeric columns: min, max, mean
- Options: `--columns <names>` (limit to specific columns), `--delimiter <char>`, `--sample <n>` (sample N rows for large files)

### `sak csv validate [files...]`
Check CSV structure and report errors.
- Detect: inconsistent column counts, unclosed quotes, encoding issues
- Exit 0 if valid, exit 1 if errors found
- Report line number and error description
- Options: `--delimiter <char>`, `--strict` (reject rows with mismatched column counts), `--quiet`

## Implementation Notes

- Follow `src/fs/mod.rs` dispatch pattern: `CsvCommand` enum + `run()` dispatcher
- Each command in its own file under `src/csv/`
- All operations are strictly read-only
- Use `csv` crate for parsing
- Output through `BoundedWriter` with `--limit` support
- Read from stdin when no files given (like `fs cut`)
- Respect exit code conventions: 0 = results, 1 = no results/invalid, 2 = error
- Errors to stderr prefixed with `sak: error:`

## Dependencies

- `csv` crate

title: Add fs enhancements: tree, stat, head, tail, wc commands
description:
Add new commands to the existing `fs` domain.

## Commands

### `sak fs tree [path]`
Display directory structure as an indented tree.
- Options: `--max-depth <n>`, `--hidden` (include dotfiles), `--dirs-only`, `--limit`
- Skip default ignored directories (.git, target, node_modules, etc.)
- Show file counts at the bottom: "N directories, M files"
- Deterministic: sorted alphabetically at each level

### `sak fs stat <path...>`
Show file/directory metadata.
- Output: size, permissions, modified time, type (file/dir/symlink)
- For directories: include entry count
- Options: `--format <human|json>` (human-readable sizes vs raw bytes)
- Tab-separated fields for parseability

### `sak fs head <file> [n]`
Show first N lines of a file (default 10).
- Essentially `sak fs read` with `--lines 1-N` but more ergonomic
- Options: `--bytes <n>` (first N bytes instead of lines), `--no-line-numbers`

### `sak fs tail <file> [n]`
Show last N lines of a file (default 10).
- Options: `--bytes <n>` (last N bytes), `--no-line-numbers`

### `sak fs wc [files...]`
Count lines, words, bytes in files.
- Output: `lines<TAB>words<TAB>bytes<TAB>filename`
- With multiple files: show totals row
- Options: `--lines` / `--words` / `--bytes` (show only selected counts)
- Read from stdin when no files given

## Implementation Notes

- Add new command files under `src/fs/`
- Update `src/fs/mod.rs` to add new variants to `FsCommand` enum
- Reuse existing `output.rs` utilities (BoundedWriter, format_line_number, relative_path)
- Reuse directory-skipping logic from glob/grep
- head/tail could potentially be thin wrappers around read logic
- All operations are strictly read-only
- Lower priority (3) since core fs commands already exist

title: feat(sqlite): add sqlite domain foundation behind opt-in cargo feature
description:
## Context

SAK currently has data-peek domains for `json` and `config` (TOML/YAML/plist) but no way to look inside SQLite databases. SQLite `.db` files show up constantly in app data, browser history, analytics dumps, and CI fixtures, and LLMs often need to peek into one read-only.

SQLite is a strong fit for SAK because read-only enforcement is OS-enforced via `SQLITE_OPEN_READONLY` plus `PRAGMA query_only=ON` — *cleaner* than the convention-plus-grep approach used by k8s. `rusqlite` maps cleanly to `serde_json::Value` so output flows through `BoundedWriter` the same way every other domain does. The dep can be cargo-feature-gated (`sqlite`) the same way `k8s` is.

This issue is the **foundation** for the sqlite domain. Six command issues depend on it (tables, schema, query, count, dump, info) and should be picked up only after this lands.

## Cargo feature

Add a new optional feature `sqlite` in `Cargo.toml`. **Recommendation: opt-in, not in `default = [...]`** until size and build-time impact are measured. The k8s feature (which is on by default) roughly doubles the release binary; we should not stack a second large feature into the default build without measuring first.

Use `rusqlite` with the `bundled` feature (compiles libsqlite3 from source, no system dep). Mirror the gating shape from `Cargo.toml:24-25` and `Cargo.toml:43-49`:

\`\`\`toml
[features]
default = [\"k8s\"]
k8s = [\"dep:kube\", \"dep:k8s-openapi\", \"dep:tokio\", \"dep:http\"]
sqlite = [\"dep:rusqlite\"]

[dependencies]
rusqlite = { version = \"0.32\", default-features = false, features = [\"bundled\"], optional = true }
\`\`\`

(Pin a specific 0.x — don't track latest.)

## Domain dispatch

Mirror the shape of `src/k8s/mod.rs`:

- `pub mod client;`
- `pub enum SqliteCommand` (empty for now — commands are added by dependent issues)
- `pub fn run(cmd: &SqliteCommand) -> Result<ExitCode>` — sync, no tokio runtime needed (sqlite is sync)

Wire it into `src/main.rs` next to the existing domain dispatches, gated on `#[cfg(feature = \"sqlite\")]`.

## Read-only enforcement (chokepoint)

`src/sqlite/client.rs` is the chokepoint that owns every `rusqlite::Connection`. All connection opening must go through:

\`\`\`rust
pub fn open_readonly(path: &Path) -> Result<Connection>
\`\`\`

which calls:

\`\`\`rust
Connection::open_with_flags(
    path,
    OpenFlags::SQLITE_OPEN_READ_ONLY | OpenFlags::SQLITE_OPEN_URI,
)
\`\`\`

and then immediately runs \`PRAGMA query_only = ON;\` as defense in depth.

Add a small \`query_rows\` helper in \`client.rs\` that returns rows as \`Vec<Vec<rusqlite::types::Value>>\` (or similar) so other modules under \`src/sqlite/\` never need to touch \`Connection\` directly.

## Chokepoint grep test

Mirror the k8s pattern from \`src/k8s/client.rs\`. Add a unit test in \`src/sqlite/client.rs\` that walks every other file under \`src/sqlite/\` and asserts none of them mention:

- \`rusqlite::Connection\`
- \`.execute(\` or \`.execute_batch(\` (mutation methods)
- \`Connection::open\` in any form

Only \`client.rs\` may mention these.

## Read-only behavior test

Add a unit test that:

1. Creates a temp sqlite file with a table and a row using a *separate* writable connection.
2. Opens it through \`open_readonly\`.
3. Asserts that an attempted \`INSERT\` / \`UPDATE\` / \`DELETE\` / \`CREATE TABLE\` through that connection returns an error.

(The mutation attempt has to go through \`client.rs\` itself, since other files cannot touch \`Connection\` per the grep test.)

## Documentation

Update \`CLAUDE.md\`:

- **Architecture** section: mention the \`sqlite\` domain alongside json/config and the read-only chokepoint enforcement story. Note that sqlite's enforcement is stronger than k8s's (OS flag + PRAGMA, not just convention).
- **File Layout**: add \`src/sqlite/\` to the example layout.
- **Build & Test**: add \`cargo build --features sqlite\` to the example commands.
- **Gotchas**: note the \`bundled\` rusqlite feature compiles libsqlite3 from source (longer first build, no system dep needed).

Update \`README.md\` if it lists domains.

## Files

**Create:**
- \`src/sqlite/mod.rs\`
- \`src/sqlite/client.rs\`

**Modify:**
- \`Cargo.toml\` — add \`sqlite\` feature and \`rusqlite\` optional dep
- \`src/main.rs\` — wire up the new domain (gated on feature)
- \`CLAUDE.md\` — Architecture, File Layout, Build & Test, Gotchas
- \`README.md\` — if it lists domains

## Acceptance

- \`cargo test\` (default features) passes.
- \`cargo test --no-default-features\` passes.
- \`cargo test --no-default-features --features sqlite\` passes.
- \`cargo test --all-features\` passes.
- \`cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets\` clean.
- \`cargo fmt\` clean.
- Read-only behavior test passes.
- Chokepoint grep test passes.
- \`cargo run --features sqlite -- sqlite --help\` dispatches (lists no commands yet — that is fine, dependent issues add them).
- **Minor** version bump in \`Cargo.toml\` (new domain).

title: feat(k8s): images and env commands
description:
## Context

Third of four issues adding the `k8s` domain. Adds two targeted extractors for the most common LLM-useful queries against pod-bearing resources: \"what images are running\" and \"what env vars / secret refs does this thing have.\"

Both commands walk container specs, so they share a single walker module (\`src/k8s/containers.rs\`) which is **pure** and unit-testable on \`serde_json::Value\` fixtures with no cluster needed. This is the issue with the most testable logic in the whole k8s domain.

**Depends on sak-llm-k7e (foundation).** Can be developed in parallel with sak-llm-ovb (kinds + get).

## Shared walker — \`src/k8s/containers.rs\`

A single function used by both commands:

\`\`\`rust
pub struct ContainerView<'a> {
    pub namespace: Option<&'a str>,
    pub name: &'a str,            // resource name
    pub container: &'a str,       // container name
    pub image: &'a str,
    pub env: &'a [serde_json::Value],
}

pub fn walk_containers(value: &serde_json::Value) -> impl Iterator<Item = ContainerView<'_>>;
\`\`\`

The walker handles two shapes:
- Pod: \`spec.containers[*]\`
- Pod-template owners (Deployment, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob, ReplicaSet): \`spec.template.spec.containers[*]\`
- CronJob has an extra hop: \`spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.containers[*]\`

Walker only — no networking. **Unit tests on hand-built JSON fixtures live alongside the walker** and run on every \`cargo test --features k8s\` invocation.

## Commands

### \`sak k8s images [kind]\`

Walks container specs across resources of \`kind\` (default \`pods\`) and emits one line per container.

Args:
- \`[kind]\` — defaults to \`pods\`; resolved via \`discovery.rs\`
- \`-n, --namespace\` / \`-A, --all-namespaces\`
- \`-l, --selector\`
- \`--limit\`

Output:
- Format: \`namespace/name<TAB>container<TAB>image\`, one line per container, sorted by \`(namespace, name, container)\`.
- Through \`BoundedWriter\`.

### \`sak k8s env <kind> <name>\`

Walks env vars on a single named resource. Restricted to kinds that have a pod template:

Allowed kinds: Pod, Deployment, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob, ReplicaSet. Any other kind → clear error message at the front of the command (no silent no-op).

Args:
- \`<kind>\`, \`<name>\`
- \`-n, --namespace\`
- \`--limit\`

Output: \`container<TAB>name<TAB>value-or-ref\` per env var, where \`value-or-ref\` is:
- For literal values: the string value
- For \`configMapKeyRef\`: \`cm:<configmap-name>/<key>\`
- For \`secretKeyRef\`: \`secret:<secret-name>/<key>\`
- For \`fieldRef\`: \`field:<fieldPath>\`
- For \`resourceFieldRef\`: \`resource:<resource>\`

**Read-only**: \`env\` does **not** dereference secret/configmap refs to fetch their actual values — it only reports the references. This keeps the command pure and avoids accidentally surfacing secret material.

## Files

**Create:**
- \`src/k8s/containers.rs\` (with extensive unit tests on fixture JSON)
- \`src/k8s/images.rs\`
- \`src/k8s/env.rs\`

**Modify:**
- \`src/k8s/mod.rs\` — add \`Images\` and \`Env\` variants, wire dispatch.

## Acceptance criteria

- \`cargo test --features k8s\` passes, including comprehensive \`containers.rs\` unit tests covering Pod, Deployment, CronJob, and unknown shapes.
- Chokepoint grep test in \`client.rs\` still passes (\`images.rs\`/\`env.rs\` must not import \`kube::Api\`).
- \`cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets\` clean.
- Manual integration tests against a real cluster:
  - \`cargo run --features k8s -- k8s images\` (pods, current namespace)
  - \`cargo run --features k8s -- k8s images deployment -A\`
  - \`cargo run --features k8s -- k8s env deployment foo -n bar\`
  - \`cargo run --features k8s -- k8s env service some-svc\` → clear error
- Patch-level version bump.
close_reason:
Landed sak k8s images and sak k8s env, plus the shared pure walker.

Walker (src/k8s/containers.rs): pure function over serde_json::Value, no networking, no kube types. Recognizes three shapes — Pod (spec.containers), pod-template owners (spec.template.spec.containers: Deployment/StatefulSet/DaemonSet/Job/ReplicaSet), and CronJob (spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.containers). Walked in specificity order so a CronJob with a redundant inner template still picks the right containers. Yields ContainerView<'a> borrowing namespace/name/container/image/env from the input. Eight unit tests cover all three shapes, unknown shapes, missing metadata, env presence/absence, and the CronJob-precedence pathological case — all run on every cargo test --features k8s.

images: <kind=pods> with -n/-A/-l/--limit. Same scope-validation as get (cluster-scoped + --namespace -> hard error). Materializes (ns,name,container,image) tuples, sorts, emits 'namespace/name<TAB>container<TAB>image' through BoundedWriter. Defaults to pods so 'sak k8s images' is the zero-arg LLM-friendly form.

env: <kind> <name> with -n/--limit. Resolves the kind, then enforces ALLOWED_KINDS = {Pod, Deployment, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob, ReplicaSet} — anything else is a clear bail with the supported list. get_dyn returning Ok(None) maps to exit 1. render_env_value handles literal value:, configMapKeyRef -> cm:<name>/<key>, secretKeyRef -> secret:<name>/<key>, fieldRef -> field:<fieldPath>, resourceFieldRef -> resource:<resource>, and an unknown-shape '?' fallback so output is always well-formed. Six unit tests on render_env_value cover all five shapes plus the fallback. Read-only by design: secret/configmap refs are NOT dereferenced — surfacing secret material would defeat the point of a read-only LLM tool.

Chokepoint preserved: neither images.rs nor env.rs nor containers.rs imports kube::Api; the grep test in client.rs still passes. images.rs and env.rs touch only ApiResource/ApiCapabilities/ListParams/Scope through the chokepoint; containers.rs is pure serde_json.

Verified end-to-end against KUBECONFIG=../home-ops/kubeconfig:
- k8s images -A --limit 8 -> sorted output, BoundedWriter truncation notice, exit 0
- k8s images deployment -n cert-manager -> walks Deployment templates (cert-manager, cainjector, webhook)
- k8s env deployment cert-manager -n cert-manager -> 'cert-manager-controller<TAB>POD_NAMESPACE<TAB>field:metadata.namespace'
- k8s env service some-svc -> 'kind "Service" has no pod template — sak k8s env only supports: ...', exit 2

cargo build, cargo build --features k8s, cargo test, cargo test --features k8s, cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets, cargo fmt --check all clean. Cargo.toml bumped 0.5.1 -> 0.5.2 (patch bump for new commands within an existing domain).

title: feat(sqlite): info command
description:
## Context

Database-level metadata in one shot. Page size, encoding, schema version, journal mode, integrity check status, file size on disk. Useful when an LLM is handed an unfamiliar \`.db\` and needs to know whether it is corrupt, what version it speaks, and roughly how big it is before running anything else.

Lower priority than the other commands — most LLM workflows will not reach for it daily, but when they need it nothing else gives the answer in one call.

Depends on the sqlite domain foundation issue.

## Command

### \`sak sqlite info <db>\`

Args:
- \`<db>\` — path to the SQLite database file
- \`--limit <n>\`

PRAGMAs to read:
- \`page_size\`
- \`page_count\`
- \`encoding\`
- \`application_id\`
- \`user_version\`
- \`schema_version\`
- \`journal_mode\`
- \`integrity_check\` — runs the actual check; can be slow on huge files. Document this in \`long_about\`.
- File size on disk via \`std::fs::metadata\` (key: \`file_size_bytes\`)
- Computed: \`total_pages_bytes = page_size * page_count\`

## Output

\`key<TAB>value\`, one per line, sorted by key (sak default deterministic ordering). Through \`BoundedWriter\`.

Example:

\`\`\`
application_id  0
encoding        UTF-8
file_size_bytes 49152
integrity_check ok
journal_mode    delete
page_count      12
page_size       4096
schema_version  3
total_pages_bytes       49152
user_version    0
\`\`\`

## Exit codes

- 0 always when the database opens, even if \`integrity_check\` reports errors (the integrity status is part of the output, not the exit signal)
- 2 if the file does not exist or is not a SQLite database

## Implementation notes

- All PRAGMA reads via the \`client::query_rows_dyn\` helper from the foundation / query issue. PRAGMAs return one-row results that pull cleanly into a \`String\`.
- Use \`std::fs::metadata\` for the file size — no need to go through sqlite for that.
- Sort keys alphabetically before emitting.

## Files

**Create:**
- \`src/sqlite/info.rs\`

**Modify:**
- \`src/sqlite/mod.rs\` — add \`Info\` variant and dispatch.

## Acceptance

- \`cargo test --features sqlite\` passes — unit test over a temp database checking that all expected keys appear, that \`integrity_check\` reports \`ok\`, and that \`file_size_bytes\` matches \`std::fs::metadata\`.
- Chokepoint grep test still passes.
- \`cargo clippy --all-features --all-targets\` clean.
- Manual: \`cargo run --features sqlite -- sqlite info some.db\`.
- Patch-level version bump.

title: feat(k8s): kinds and get commands
description:
## Context

Second of four issues adding the `k8s` domain. Builds the first user-facing commands on top of the foundation from sak-llm-k7e.
